Bel and Nebo cannot carry themselves, let alone their worshipers. Isaiah opens with an image so cutting that it demands we stop and see it clearly: the gods of Babylon—the very powers Israel feared in exile—must be *carried* by beasts and exhausted animals. The idols are a burden. Those who crafted them poured out gold and silver, hired skilled workers, fashioned these objects with human hands, then lifted them onto their own shoulders to transport them from place to place. And there they stand, immobile and silent. A person cries out in desperation, and the idol offers nothing—no answer, no deliverance, no help. This is what Israel has been tempted toward: trusting in things that cannot even support their own weight. The contrast could not be starker. God calls out to Jacob and Israel: "I have sustained you from the womb. I carried you since birth. Even when you grow old and gray, I will be the same, and I will carry you." The One speaking is not transported on your shoulders. He transports you.

But notice what makes this promise so formidable: it comes to transgressors. Isaiah does not address the faithful remnant as if they had remained loyal throughout exile. He calls them stubborn people, far removed from righteousness. They have stumbled into idolatry not by accident but by choice, turning toward what can be touched and shaped rather than toward the God they cannot see. Yet God declares His purpose to this very people: "I declare the end from the beginning. My purpose will stand, and all My good pleasure I will accomplish." He is not promising ease or comfort. He is calling forth a conqueror—a bird of prey from the east, a man for His purpose from a far-off land. This is Cyrus, who will come and overturn Babylon's power. God's faithfulness does not mean your enemies will not advance or your circumstances will not change. It means that behind all history, beyond all earthly powers, God's plan unfolds exactly as He determined it before time began.

What God brings near to this rebellious people is not wrath alone—it is His righteousness and His salvation. He will grant salvation to Zion and adorn Israel with His splendor. This is not because Israel deserved it or finally earned it through repentance. It is because God's purpose stands. His good pleasure will be accomplished. The gods of Babylon crumble into captivity while the God of Israel brings His people salvation and adorns them with His own glory. As you read this today, ask yourself honestly: Where do you carry burdens that only God can bear? Where have you been tempted to trust in what human hands have made—whether literal idols or the subtle ones of achievement, security, and control? The word to you is the same word to Israel: Remember what happened long ago. I am God, and there is no other. Listen. Trust. Be carried.
